text: The surgeon begins the flap creation from the anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS) to the medial umbilical ligaments, followed by medial dissection. The bone is visible, and lateral dissection is initiated with sweeping of cremasteric tissue. The chord structures and vas deferens come into view.
The surgeon reduces the hernia sac, which includes some preperitoneal fat, and proceeds to parietalize the cord structures.

The surgeon exposes the femoral defect and continues dissection down to the outer hyperspace. The structures are being parietalized to ensure the mesh sits flat against the sidewall, preventing clamshelling, with further parietalization extending down to the solace.

The surgeon examines the anatomy of the myopectineal orifice and identifies a cord lipoma. Fatty tissue is observed entering the inguinal canal, with the iliopubic tract visible. Herniated fat is noted entering a separate defect lateral to the internal ring, which is then excised. The surgeon sweeps back tissue, revealing nodal tissue over the iliac vessels.

The surgeon encounters increased bleeding during dissection and decides to leave the tissue in place after reducing the herniated fat. A 16x12 mesh is positioned over the myopectineal orifice, and once satisfied with the placement, the surgeon proceeds to close the flap.
